'''Clinton James Te-Whata Toopi''' (born 29 February 1980) is a New Zealand former professional [[rugby league]] and [[rugby union]] footballer who played in the 2000s and 2010s. A New Zealand international representative {{rlp|ce}}, he played his club football in the [[National Rugby League]] for the [[New Zealand Warriors]] and the [[Gold Coast Titans]], and in the [[Super League]] for the [[Leeds Rhinos]]. Clinton began coaching the Helensvale Hornets in 2021 and guided them to victory in the 2022 Gold Coast A Grade Competition.
Toopi won the 2007 [[Super League]] grand final with [[Leeds Rhinos]] over [[St Helens R.F.C.|Saints]] 33-6.


==Rugby league career==
Toopi was born in [[Stratford, New Zealand]], and began his career playing [[rugby league]] with the junior ranks of the Mt Wellington and [[Otahuhu Leopards]] clubs. He played for Auckland South in the 1999 provincial competition.<ref>Coffey, John and Bernie Wood ''Auckland, 100 years of rugby league, 1909–2009'', 2009. {{ISBN|978-1-86969-366-4}}, p.323.</ref> His [[National Rugby League|first grade]] career began with the then [[Auckland Warriors]], débuting against the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]] on 2 May 1999. He went on to play 119 games for the club, including the [[2002 NRL Grand Final]] loss to the [[Sydney Roosters]].

In 2000 Toopi was part of the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|Aotearoa Maori]] side that played at the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup|World Cup]].

Toopi represented New Zealand on twelve occasions between 2001 and 2005. Toopi played for the Kiwis 14 times. He scored three tries in one match against Australia in the 2005 Tri Nations.

On 31 May 2006 [[Leeds Rhinos]] announced the capture of Toopi for the 2007 season as a replacement for [[Chev Walker]]. On 17 July he was granted an early release from the New Zealand Warriors so he could join the Rhinos for the rest of the season, this was granted the day before the deadline to register players in Super League. He made his début on 4 August 2006 against Hull FC.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://warriors.sitesuite.ws/plugins/newsfeed.cgi?rm=content&plugin_data_id=13734 |title=Warriors Website - News |accessdate=2006-07-18 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070311062357/http://warriors.sitesuite.ws/plugins/newsfeed.cgi?rm=content&plugin_data_id=13734 |archivedate=11 March 2007}}</ref>

==Rugby union career==
On 23 August 2008 Leeds released Toopi, due to a knee reconstruction that would keep him out of action for 9 months. He attracted interest from the Japanese rugby union club [[Sanyo Wild Knights]], and expressed interest in joining the [[Manly Sea Eagles]] in the [[National Rugby League]] competition however he did not secure a contract.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/sport/rugby/news/2315671/Japanese-scouts-eye-Toopi|title=Japanese scouts eye Toopi|first=Aaron|last=Lawton|date=5 April 2009|website=Stuff.co.nz}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/newslocal/manly-daily |title=Former Manly mayor calls it a day |website=Goldcoast.com.au |accessdate=2017-04-25}}</ref>

Toopi instead returned to New Zealand and switched to [[rugby union]], joining the [[Whakatane Marist]] team in the [[Eastern Bay]] [[rugby union]] competition. On 18 June 2009 it was announced that he had made the [[Bay of Plenty Rugby Union]]'s squad for the [[Air New Zealand Cup]].<ref>[http://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=10579258&ref=rss Recycled Toopi into mix for Steamers] ''Rotorua Daily Post'', 18 June 2009</ref> He agreed to join the Gold Coast Titans for 2010.

==Return to league==
During the 2010 pre-season Toopi trialed with the [[Gold Coast Titans]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.goldcoast.com.au/article/2009/11/14/158391_gold-coast-titans.html |title=Elgey still preferred five-eighth |website=Goldcoast.com.au |accessdate=2017-04-25}}</ref> He did enough to impress management and earn a one-year contract.<ref>[http://www.nrl.com/NewsViews/LatestNews/NewsArticle/tabid/10874/newsId/57762/Default.aspx Toopi signs NRL deal with Titans] ''NRL.com'', 5 March 2010</ref> This was later extended for the 2011 season. He retired at the end of 2011.<ref>[http://www.couriermail.com.au/sport/clinton-toopi-set-to-retire-after-fainting-in-gold-coast-titans-game-nrl-game-against-melbourne-storm/story-e6frep5o-1226114723610 Clinton Toopi set to retire after fainting in Gold Coast Titans game NRL game against Melbourne Storm] ''Couriermail.com'', 15 August 2011</ref>

Toopi again played for the New Zealand Māori in 2010, captaining the side against [[England national rugby league team|England]].<ref>[http://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=10681035&ref=rss Maori draw with England] ''New Zealand Herald'', 16 October 2010</ref>

== Match Fit ==
In 2023, Toopi participated in season 3 of ''Match Fit'', where former rugby players return to play against the Australian counterparts. He joined in the first season that featured former [[rugby league]] stars.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Match Fit Season 3 Ep 1 {{!}} SPORT {{!}} ThreeNow |url=https://www.threenow.co.nz/shows/match-fit/season-3-ep-1/S2339-580/M70717-275 |access-date=2023-04-13 |website=www.threenow.co.nz}}</ref> On episode 3, he revealed he currently resides in [[Gold Coast, Queensland|Gold Coast, Australia]]. Throughout the first third of the 15-week programme, his heart rate kept topping out even though he feels no ill effects, which suggest he has lower than expected aerobic endurance, he thought it was due to [[jet lag]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Match Fit Season 3 Ep 3 {{!}} SPORT {{!}} ThreeNow |url=https://www.threenow.co.nz/shows/match-fit/season-3-ep-3/S2339-580/M71150-618 |access-date=2023-05-03 |website=www.threenow.co.nz}}</ref>

In 2024, he returned for ''Match Fit: Union vs. League''. While [[Pita Alatini]] and [[Carlos Spencer]] went -15 for metabolic age vs. biological age for the best possible result, he, [[Lesley Vainikolo]] and Henry Fa'afili started out +15, which is the worst possible result.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Match Fit Season 4 Ep 1 {{!}} SPORT {{!}} ThreeNow |url=https://www.threenow.co.nz/shows/match-fit/season-4-ep-1/S2339-580/M87456-547 |access-date=2024-07-29 |website=www.threenow.co.nz}}</ref> He admitted he didn't take last season seriously enough until he went -15 for metabolic age for the second initial test in a row.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Match Fit Season 4 Ep 2 {{!}} SPORT {{!}} ThreeNow |url=https://www.threenow.co.nz/shows/match-fit/season-4-ep-2/S2339-580/M87690-532 |access-date=2024-08-01 |website=www.threenow.co.nz}}</ref> During the IronMaori Triathlon, he was paired in the swim leg with [[Louis Anderson]] despite being scared of swimming in open water.
